item.title: What a difference this must make!! Adorable!
item.description: Edit ( because, quite rightly, people had questions) on investigation, the book is Portrait of Clare (1927) by Francis Brett Young, and it's the library in Suffolk UK... But in case any clarification is needed, I'm not the original op, I just saw it on another site and thought it was great and worth sharing!
item.dc:creator: DumpingSh1tandBeingKind2

item.title: What just happened?
item.description: Was at the gym with the tinier doing push day, and she's just moved up a weight on bench, so tiny octopus was spotting and encouraging her through her sets. As we were changing weights to go from a bench press to shoulder presses, the lady on the bench in front of us stopped tiny octopus to say that she loved the relationship he has with his daughter. It was nice, only slightly made awkward by the tinier asking him afterwards if she was flirting with him. To which tiny octopus replied, "I DON'T KNOW." Which he really doesn't. He's just chalking it up to some lady being nice and it being a random act of kindness that made tiny octopus smile a bit.
item.dc:creator: TinyOctopus

pretty sure that won't stop anyone.. greetings online friends
seems appropriate
mmm hmm
might want to put a little tape on it before the flight
i hope you are taking notes, this is how it's done
very cool markings
how is it growing w/out food? so many other questions
who the heck cares what's in the tablets, it gives you fire breath
happy MIM to all those who partake
hopefully the mods scroll by this one
big turnout
probably bc they didnt have reeses pieces back when we found olives?
it's in there somewhere
this dump does not have any hinges
wait what
no
the end
that’s it for me.. have a great night gang!!

item.title: Is China about to collapse?
item.description:
I'm going to be frank: China is in very deep shit. China's economy is in crisis, its population is aging faster than any other nation in recorded history, the government is more centralized and tyrannical than it's been since Mao Zedong tiptoed to hell in the 1970s, and it's more diplomatically isolated than ever before. Will things get worse or will they turn it around? And if things get worse, just how bad will it get? Let's take a look.
Background: China's glorious rises and terrible falls Imperial China can be said to have begun around 1600 years before the birth of Christ, only ending, once and for all, in 1912. In all those centuries, there were multiple dynasties, and they all followed essentially the same tragic script. A fiery youth, where a series of gifted emperors unite the country, bring peace and stability, the empire grows larger, stronger, richer, the lives of ordinary people improve, things are great. But then, as always happens with hereditary succession, later emperors have fewer abilities, less dedication, and more easily distracted with praise, luxuries and the ever-present harem. Governance declines, corruption grows, poverty increases, and perhaps most importantly, the throne is no longer able to guard against invasion, banditry, or to provide assistance in the event of natural disasters. Uprisings ensue, warlordism increases, and the one-great dynasty collapses. A couple of times, dynasties were replaced by powerful foreign invaders, but those invading dynasties repeated the old pattern. And when a dynasty falls the result is what we today might call a "failed state." Mass poverty, chaos, crime, disunity -- nightmares. Sometimes these periods last for centuries. The last one started in the late 1700s and lasted until 1949 -- or, perhaps more honestly, until the late 1970s. There are still people in China who remember what it was like to live with foreign invasion, mass narcotics addiction, crime, disasters, savagery, genocide. Please believe me when I say the spectre of societal collapse continues to loom very large in the hearts and minds of everyone in China, including its leaders. It happened before and it can happen again.
After Mao: The social contract of Deng Xiaopeng Deng was a really interesting guy (I highly recommend reading a biography if you have the time). For Mao, the Chinese Revolution was a goal in of itself; he considered permanent revolution to be the highest level a country could attain. But for Deng, who had joined the revolution in the 1920s, it had a very clear goal: to unite China and raise living standards. He famously said it didn't matter if a cat was black or white as long as it caught mice. In other words, capitalist economics were fine if they improved people's lives. "To get rich is glorious," he said. Truly shocking so soon after Mao's crazy time! And so a new deal was struck. In place of Mao's "politics first" approach, Deng basically said that as long as no one questioned the rule of the Communist Party, the people of China were free to invest, trade, even travel. In other words, "keep us in power and we'll make you rich beyond your wildest dreams." The result was decades of growth unlike anything the world has ever seen, hundreds of millions of people were lifted out of abject, starving poverty, and in less than one generation China went from an almost completely rural country with dusty, dreary cities to an industrialized, urbanized superpower with massive, glittering, modern, high-tech cities. The people paid a high price for all this. That urban migration caused a hell of a lot of suffering and struggle, and there were (and still are) huge pockets of poverty, the environmental costs have been damn near catastrophic (the Yellow River, once called "China's Sorrow" for its habit of flooding, no longer even reaches the sea due to over-exploitation of water). But living standards are damn-near utopian compared to where they were in the 70s.
Growth fueled by debt, debt, and more debt China has amazed the world by building a truly staggering amount of stuff. High-speed rail, bridges and highways through remote terrain, a train up to freaking Tibet. Airports, seaports, subways, oh my! Of course this is nothing compared to the mind-boggling amount of real estate construction nationwide. None of it is the result of careful study into the economic viability of any of it; basically it was orders from the national government. And when I say "orders," I mean orders. Banks were ordered to lend and local governments (which funded most of the infrastructure) were ordered to borrow. Property developers, spurred by real estate valuations that make sticker prices in London and San Fran look like penny stocks, went on borrowing sprees that are hard to comprehend. I could say that Chinese banks have racked up $9 trillion in debt. I could add that local governments have somehow piled up close to $13 trillion in debt. I could throw in Chinese corporate debt, which is officially pegged at somewhere in the neighbourhood of $29 trillion. What the heck, let's add the national government's debt, which is a mere $4 trillion. Those numbers add up to around $55 trillion. That's around 300% of China's GDP. And let's add one last tidbit: these figures are the official numbers as released by the Chinese government. However they constantly lie about every point of data, have for years. It's widely believed the actual debt numbers are FAR worse. Twice as bad? Three times? Ten times? Believe me, some of the estimates venture into incredibly horrendous territory.
China's ugly debt trap Xi Jinping's cornerstone policy, the Belt and Road Initiative (BRI), had a very simple goal: approach nations that have been rejected by traditional lenders due to poverty and corruption and lend them ungodly sums of money. That money would be used to pay for big and shiny infrastructure projects that would help local economies -- and extend China's influence all over the world. Unfortunately, BRI has turned into a nasty debt trap. Projects remain uncompleted, governments and currencies in multiple countries are in chaos due to those Chinese loans they can't possibly pay back. This in turn has placed additional burden on China's already-strained finances. Once again, reckless governance at the very top.
China is aging -- rapidly Like so much of the world, China experienced a massive baby boom from the late 1940s to the mid-1960s. It wasn't unusual for women to have seven kids or more. It was kind of astonishing. The population grew so quickly that China's leaders worried it would lead to famine, so they instituted a one-child policy -- no one was allowed to have more than one child. Well, that policy worked brilliantly. It also coincided with the gargantuan shift in population from mostly agricultural lives to modern, urban lives, which always reduces the birth rate. All this has combined to create a collapse in China's birth rate. What this means is that China's population has begun to decline. How much will it decline? Well, estimates vary. China's population could be reduced by half by the end of the century. Some forecasts are worse; imagine China and the USA having roughly equal populations. But it's not just about declining numbers, which could, in theory, create labour shortages. It's also about the aging of the population. It's quite possible that within a few years, there will be more old people in the country than young workers able to support them. Many countries are faced with similar situations, and it's a real problem. Some people subscribe to the demographics-as-destiny viewpoint and insist China will no longer exist as a functioning state within a decade, but I'm not so sure. One thing that is for certain, however, is that it will place a heavy strain on China's economy and finances. Remember, they're entering this rapidly-aging period of history with severely troubled finances. How are they going to support all those old folk?
The biggie: China's bonkers real estate crisis Real estate bubbles have been around awhile. Cheap money leads to heavy construction, which people snap up because money is cheap. That spurs developers to borrow heavily so they can build more, and sell more. Then pop! goes the bubble, prices collapse, debts overwhelm developers, which go bankrupt, individual homebuyers lose their shirts. It's not unusual for property bubbles to send national economies into serious recession. Well think of China like Texas -- everything's bigger there. Just how big was the real estate bubble in China? Well, I could throw out official numbers but that's a waste of time because they're far worse than official statements. Okay, here's a number for you. Developers overbuilt by a lot. A LOT. How much? By some estimates, there's enough empty housing units to house 3 billion people. That's double the population of the country. Imagine if America had 600,000,000 empty housing units. Totally bonkers. Whole cities are sitting empty. It's even worse than a potential collapse of housing values; in a country where the stock market is laughably fixed, foreign currency trades are all but illegal and Chinese cash is more of a coupon than an actual currency, housing has long been the principal investment for Chinese citizens. Take that away and you bankrupt an entire generation. It threatens to completely reverse decades of hard-won economic progress.
Last up: Emperor Xi's centralization of power It's kind of insane how Xi Jinping has brought China to the brink of catastrophe. As a power-hungry tyrant who sees himself and a new Mao Zedong, Xi has concentrated so much power into his hands that he's basically established one-man rule. Years of purges of any opposition, real or potential, has ensured that no one is left to discuss, debate or even ask questions. If Chairman Xi wants to set fire to trillions of dollars on useless projects, that's what's going to happen. If Chairman Xi wants to burn all the diplomatic relationships that China depends on as sources for foreign investment, that's how it will be. But one thing about one-man rule -- it's fragile. There is plenty of reporting as to how much this guy is hated by people with money and power. The problem is, years of purges means that chopping off the head could leave the body to die. There's a similar situation in Russia. These guys dominate the playground for a few years then leave it in ruins when they go. So will China collapse, as it has so many times before? Hard to say. But as someone who once lived in China, loves China, studies China, I gotta say: I'm worried. Note: I made a previous post on how Xi Jinping's "Wolf Warrior Diplomacy" destroyed China's reputation on the world stage. You can find it here: https://imgur.com/gallery/n78ouU9
item.dc:creator: Huor

item.title: Remember this on the 5th of November
item.description: There are exactly 300 days until Decision 2024 and there is lot's to be done! First things first .... register to vote / check your registration ... vote.org Secondly get involved, here are some handy links https://runforsomething.net/run/candidate-support-system/ https://www.turnoutpac.org/ https://justicedemocrats.com/ https://ballotpedia.org/Election_results,_2022:_Uncontested_races_by_state Why: https://www.axios.com/2023/11/13/trump-loyalists-2024-presidential-election EVERYTHING IS ON THE LINE!

item.title: IM SO EXCITED
item.description: Recently I've been getting a lot of hate from an old 'friend' for being lesbian (we hadn't had contact for a few years, I don't even know how they got my number) but the news somehow got to them and they started blowing up my phone with the stereotypical "how would your parents think", "this is not what god intended" and "I can't believe you would do this to your friends and family, me included" sort of shiz but this has massively brightened up my life:). (I tried to post this earlier but it wouldn't show up so I did it again)
item.dc:creator: QueenNova

item.title: Bottom shelf cleaning dump.
item.description:
FULL DISCLAIMER: This is a meme and a joke and I very much like a lot of Taylor's music. Can't hate someone who made a fuck ton of money doing what they love. ...and Andrew Tate is an actual piece of shit. If I could delete this photo without removing the whole dump, I would, but since I cannot, witness #10. The meme that finally gets me canceled. I'm sorry, guys.
@HileyNoteson Yoooooooooooooooooooooo
My algorithm has caught onto my love of glizzies.
@graehall
& now it's time to show off Deaf Cat Magoo (short for Oogala Magoogala)'s cleaned art studio.
Thanks for my 99th trophy.
You can catch me live on twitch.tv/bottomshelfvodka streaming with my favorite Imgurians from the Bottom Shelf Community server.
Join for the games! We've been playing Lethal Company, Red Dead Redemption 2, Pay Day 3, GTA V, Jackbox, Among Us, 7 Days to Die, Sea of Thieves, Barotrauma, Minecraft, Ultimate Chicken Horse...if there's a specific game you like, I guarantee you'll find a shitposting meme loving Imgurian there to play it with you. Join for the memes! Imgurians share memes in the meme dumpster 24/7 Join for the Awe! Pay your pet taxes in the pet tex channel Join for the NSFW memes! Join to chat with hilarious people! Join to lurk in the corners! Join for the shockingly magnificent balance of wholesomeness and chaos. discord.gg/bottomshelfvodka
Magoo desperately needing attention RIGHT AT THIS MOMENT tax.
item.dc:creator: BottomShelfVodka

item.description: Most Viral Edit: To the people that find this racist, I do apologize and would completely understand if you decided to report this post as such. However, I see nothing wrong with it since I myself am a mediocre white man with severe anger issues. Have a nice day.
item.dc:creator: AwesomeDad420

item.title: A month’s worth of meal prep to help with my sorry excuse for executive functioning
item.description:
(TL:DR - read the title) I’m ADHD but wasn’t diagnosed until a few years ago as an adult. As I’ve gotten to know this side of myself better, two things I’ve learned that are super helpful to me are 1)routine, and 2) having someone else around, which acts kind of like a subconscious reminder for me to stay on top of my executive functioning (look up body doubling if you wanna know more). Both of those just got tossed out the window though, because my spouse is gone for three months for his job and I do not want my new roommate to be chaos. Soo, I meal prepped about a month’s worth of meals to freeze so even on the nights when I can’t bring myself to cook, I at least have (subjectively) healthy and homemade options! I documented everything I did and wanted to share in case anyone else found it helpful.
I researched a bunch of different recipes that would work well frozen, went and bought all the groceries, and started on everything. In total it took me about 3 hours which I know is a lot, but I got into hyper focus mode which helped. This first dish was a ravioli lasagna (I bought the ravioli pre-made and just added ricotta, spinach, marinara sauce, and mozzarella)
It made about 5 generous portions. The plan is I’ll thaw one of these in the fridge the night before I want to have it, and then put it in a small oven safe single-serving sized dish and bake it. Minimal cleanup and almost no prep aside from thawing it. Getting them in the bags without making my kitchen look like a bloodbath from the marinara sauce was a little tricky, but I ended up putting the empty quart-sized ziplock into a tall plastic cup and folding the top of the bag over the lip of the cup to hold it open. It worked pretty well!
Next is an Alfredo tortellini. The “healthy” part is a bit of a stretch here, but at least it’s homemade. I didn’t actually bake any of these ahead of time for the most part since I’ll bake them individually, but I still used the casserole dish to help eye all the portion sizes. It made about 5 generous servings
Jalapeño chicken with potatoes. I bought a big thing of chicken breast from Costco and cooked it in the slow cooker for 6 hours until it shredded and I used that along with frozen hash browns, corn, and the jalapeños for this one. About 6 generous servings.
Broccoli and cheese with chicken. Used frozen broccoli and the same shredded chicken. Threw in the rest of the frozen hash browns from the previous recipe because I love myself, that’s why. About 6 servings.
Super excited to try these! I call them “chicken puffs”. It’s crescent roll dough with the shredded chicken, a bit of cream cheese, garlic, and onion. This is the only recipe I actually cooked ahead of time. About 5 or so servings here.
My bastardized versions of burritos. It’s low carb tortillas, low fat refried beans, extra spicy taco meat, and it’s not pictured here but I also added enchilada sauce. 5 servings.
I didn’t take an individual picture, but I had one more in the back row second to the left that I prepped for a sheet-pan meal that I can dump on a pan and pop in the oven with some olive oil and miscellaneous seasonings. It’s andouille sausage with chopped green bell pepper, jalapeño, sweet potato, and onion. Made 5 servings. And there we are! About a month of homemade meals prepped, labeled, double bagged, and ready to go. I’m not going to lie, this makes me feel better about being a bit more proactive with staying on top of stuff. Hopefully it tastes ok when I actually go to have it. Thanks for scrolling!
item.dc:creator: Sloppydrunkcatlady
